description: Two same-gender (male) piper voices having a natural back-and-forth. Some turns OVERLAP — the listener interjects ('Right.', 'Yeah, exactly.') before the speaker has fully finished, like real conversations. Same-gender + overlap is the realistic hard case the YouTube-tab demo hits in production. If diarization breaks down here, that's our bottleneck — not a corpus problem.

turns.speaker: ryan
turns.text: So I've been thinking a lot about how speech recognition systems handle interruptions.

turns.speaker: ryan
turns.text: Exactly. The classic pipeline assumes one speaker at a time, but real conversations don't work that way.

turns.speaker: joe
turns.text: Yeah, and you get all these awkward gaps when the system tries to wait for silence.

turns.speaker: ryan
turns.text: The transcription quality drops too when there's even a hint of cross talk.

turns.speaker: joe
turns.text: Have you tried any of the newer end to end models?

turns.speaker: ryan
turns.text: I've experimented with a few. They're better at robustness but still struggle with very fast turns.

turns.speaker: ryan
turns.text: Diarization is the bottleneck, especially when two speakers sound similar like we do.
